design_notes: 9 vehicle classes spanning road, animal, and air lift.
Heterogeneous by design: SHA/ECC posts are reachable only via specific vehicle types.
Surface compatibility is a hard constraint in the optimizer (Wk12). A Stallion cannot traverse SEG-TAC-12 (foot only).
Altitude derate is the hidden constraint that breaks naive resupply plans: a helo that lifts 4t at sea level lifts 1.8t at DBO.
Reliability priors (deadline rate per 1,000km) are illustrative central estimates aligned with CAG audit patterns.
Cost-per-tonne-km baselines feed Wk12 optimizer's cost dimension; relative ratios matter more than absolute INR figures.

surface_compatibility_legend.road_unpaved: BRO unpaved arterial. Heavier trucks acceptable but slower. Some derate.
surface_compatibility_legend.track: Single-vehicle dirt track. ALS/Gypsy/ALSV; mules. Stallion struggles in winter.

_meta.altitude_derate_principle: Payload reduction follows a published-physics pattern for IC engines (~3-5% per 1000m above 3000m for trucks) and a steeper curve for helos (rotorcraft thrust falls non-linearly with air density). Ground-vehicle derate is on engine power; helo derate is on lift capacity directly.

provenance_grading.A: Vehicle is publicly documented in MoD annual reports / manufacturer specs / CAG audits. Specs are public.
provenance_grading.B: Vehicle type publicly used; specific reliability numbers are illustrative central estimates.

vehicle_classes.name: Stallion 4x4 (Ashok Leyland 7.5T)
vehicle_classes.provenance_note: Stallion is publicly the workhorse of Indian Army logistics. Payload, fuel, and dimensions are from manufacturer brochure. Reliability priors are illustrative but the cold-start failure pattern is publicly discussed in CAG vehicle audits.

vehicle_classes.name: Topaz 6x6 (Ashok Leyland 10T)
vehicle_classes.provenance_note: Topaz is publicly the heavy-lift counterpart to Stallion. Fewer per brigade because they're harder on tracks; carry bulk on the trunk routes.

vehicle_classes.name: ALS Light Tactical (Tata LPTA 715)
vehicle_classes.provenance_note: Tata LPTA family is publicly documented. ALS variants are widely deployed for tactical-route resupply where Stallions struggle.

vehicle_classes.name: Gypsy / ALSV (Maruti / Mahindra)
vehicle_classes.provenance_note: Maruti Gypsy and Mahindra ALSV/MPV are publicly the standard light tactical vehicles. Used for liaison, recce, and small-cargo runs; not bulk logistics. High cost per tonne-km because payload is small.

vehicle_classes.name: Army Mule (AT Coy)
vehicle_classes.range_note: Working day. Mules need rest days; sustained ops over multiple days reduce daily km.
vehicle_classes.provenance_note: Indian Army Animal Transport units are publicly documented; Army Service Corps still maintains mule companies for SHA/ECC last-mile resupply. The publicly cited 'mule cost per tonne-km' is multiples of truck cost because of low payload, low speed, and high overhead per animal-day.

vehicle_classes.name: Civilian Porter (contract)
vehicle_classes.range_note: Sustained foot porterage over snow/ice ranges; heavily affected by fitness and acclimatization.
vehicle_classes.provenance_note: Civilian porter contracts are publicly known practice for Siachen-class final approaches. Cost per tonne-km is high because of low payload and the operational constraints of porter movement at ECC.

vehicle_classes.name: HAL Cheetah / Cheetal (Lama family)

altitude_derate_payload_curve.principle: Rotorcraft thrust falls non-linearly with air density. Cheetah/Cheetal is the only helo with a service ceiling above 6000m (Siachen ops); payload at that altitude is essentially zero.

vehicle_classes.fleet_count_typical_per_brigade: shared aviation pool, ~6-10 airframes per AOR
vehicle_classes.provenance_note: Cheetah / Cheetal is publicly the only Indian helicopter with operational ceiling for Siachen-class altitudes. Used for casevac and critical resupply where heavier helos cannot operate.

vehicle_classes.name: Mi-17 V5 (medium-lift)

altitude_derate_payload_curve.principle: Mi-17 is the workhorse for forward ALG resupply. Service ceiling around 6000m but payload severely degraded above 5000m.

vehicle_classes.fleet_count_typical_per_brigade: shared with IAF Western Air Command
vehicle_classes.provenance_note: Mi-17 V5 is publicly the medium-lift workhorse of IAF for Ladakh resupply. Operates ex-Leh forward to ALGs at DBO, Fukche, Nyoma.

vehicle_classes.name: CH-47F Chinook (heavy-lift)

altitude_derate_payload_curve.principle: Chinook can land bulk cargo at DBO when no other airframe can. Strategic asset; few airframes; tasked for highest-priority lifts.

vehicle_classes.provenance_note: Chinook fleet (15 acquired, IAF) is publicly documented. The only rotorcraft that can put real bulk into DBO ALG. Lower per-tonne-km cost than Mi-17 because of payload despite higher operating cost per hour.

vehicle_to_route_compatibility_summary.operational_note: Tiger Hill OP is foot-resupply only. Mules in summer; porters or air drop in winter. No motor vehicle option.
Summer: any truck. Winter: DSDBO frequently unusable; air bridge becomes primary.
Track narrow; heavy trucks not optimal. ALS is the workhorse here.
